therapy clinical trial?
All clinical trial treatments are termed “investigational” and therefore the true risks are not known.
When therapies are given outside of clinical trials, the provider is not required to report any side effects to the FDA, known as adverse event reporting.
During the informed consent process, patients should be given complete information on the possible risks and benefits of the treatment or procedure.
FDA regulatory oversight of these cellular therapies aims to protect patients and prevent communicable or infectious disease.
Patients and their healthcare providers may want to find out whether a cellular therapy is being studied in a clinical trial.
What are Clinical Trials?
Clinical trials are often listed on ClinicalTrials.gov.
A clinical trial is a study in people to evaluate if a certain treatment or procedure is safe and effective.
Typically, proper clinical trial studies take place at institutions with an IRB, involve qualified personnel, and follow local and federal regulations (where applicable).
Some cellular therapies have not been adequately studied through clinical trials carried out following thorough IRB review.
Some of these cellular therapies are unproven and have not been tested in appropriately conducted clinical trials.
Clinical trials may end with any one of three results: improvement in patient health outcomes, no patient improvement, or unexpected patient harm.
Informed consent has to be obtained from all participants of clinical trials.
Regenerative medicine may be defined as the process of replacing or "regenerating" human cells, tissues or organs to restore or establish normal function.
Their use should be studied in clinical trials to assess their risks and benefits.
Clinical trials follow a scientific plan or protocol.
This website explains how clinical trials help protect patients.
A clinical trial is a research study in humans to help understand if a specific medication, cellular therapy or procedure is safe and effective.
The use of HSCs for other reasons and the use of other types of stem cells should generally be considered experimental – that is, unproven in controlled clinical trials.
IRBs are administrative bodies established within an institution to protect the rights and welfare of patients who participate in clinical trial activities conducted at the institution.
As with other medical treatments, new cellular therapies are best evaluated using clinical trials.
These healthcare providers should be able to provide data and scientific resources for use of the cellular therapy and advise if the patient is being asked to take part in a clinical trial.
